senses.definition: a day that will always be remembered by an exceptionally good  thing that has happened on it

examples.x: Today is a red-letter day as we celebrate our company's 10th anniversary.
Yesterday was a red-letter day in history, marking the end of a long-standing conflict.
Next week will be a red-letter day for our family as we welcome a new addition.
They are experiencing a red-letter day as they receive recognition for their outstanding achievements.
During that period, every day felt like a red-letter day as they made significant progress towards their goals.

senses.definition: the specific moment when an event or action is scheduled to begin or take place

examples.x: We are approaching zero hour for the product launch; all preparations are in place.
At zero hour, the troops stormed the enemy's position and initiated the decisive battle.
Tomorrow will be zero hour for the negotiations; we must be well-prepared.
During the last hour, it was zero hour for the team as they executed their strategy.
By this time next week, they will be in the middle of zero hour, managing the crisis.

senses.definition: the time in which a critical event or action happens

examples.x: They are currently navigating their moment of truth, as they strive to redefine their company's mission.
The project team is in the midst of their moment of truth, collaborating to meet the impending deadline.

senses.definition: a point at which a drastic change occurs in a situation, especially one that makes it improve

examples.x: She often reflects on that turning point in her life when she decided to pursue her passion.
They are currently experiencing a turning point in their business as they expand into new markets.
His promotion at work marked a turning point in his career, leading to new opportunities and growth.
We were going through a turning point in our relationship when we realized we needed to address our communication issues.
The upcoming meeting could be a turning point for the project, determining its success or failure.

senses.definition: used for referring to the time after something has happened or been done

examples.x: They know that if they persevere through the challenges, they will eventually find themselves on the other side of success.
Right now, we are in the midst of the storm, but we are confident that we'll soon be on the other side of this difficult period.
We were pushing through the challenges, knowing that on the other side of this ordeal, we would find a sense of accomplishment.

senses.definition: to happen very closely after something else has happened

examples.x: The news of the scandal came hot on the heels of the company's annual report.
The new product launch followed close on the heels of extensive market research.
The layoffs came hard on the heels of the company's financial losses.
The government's policy changes followed close on the heels of public outcry.

senses.definition: during the time of year that a type of food, vegetable, or fruit is not naturally grown or easily found

examples.x: The grocery store imports strawberries from other countries when they are out of season locally.
He avoids buying tomatoes when they are out of season because they tend to be less flavorful.
The restaurant is currently sourcing its ingredients from local farms to ensure they are not serving anything out of season.
They are offering a limited menu during the winter months when many vegetables are out of season.
Last year, the price of avocados skyrocketed because they were out of season due to adverse weather conditions.
She missed the taste of fresh peaches during the winter months when they were out of season.

senses.definition: a day of great misfortune and unhappiness

examples.x: She always feels a sense of sadness on Black Days when reflecting on past tragedies.

senses.definition: used when something happens almost at the same time as another

examples.x: He praises her talent, but in the same breath, he criticizes her lack of experience.
They advocate for environmental sustainability, but in the next breath, they support policies that harm the environment.
He praised her work, but in the same breath, he mentioned her previous mistakes.
They enjoyed the concert, but in the next breath, they complained about the sound quality.
They were talking about the benefits of the new system, but in the next breath, they were criticizing its limitations.

senses.definition: to have no idea how much time has passed or what has happened

examples.x: When she starts reading a captivating novel, she often loses track of time and ends up staying up late.
They frequently lose track of time while playing video games, often realizing it's already dawn.
He is painting a masterpiece, and he's so absorbed in his work that he is losing track of time.
I was so engrossed in the movie that I lost track of time and missed my appointment.
They explored the city and got so caught up in the sights that they lost track of time.
They were having a lively conversation and lost track of time, not realizing how late it had become.

senses.definition: to not be busy with anything

examples.x: She often volunteers at the local shelter because she has time on her hands after retiring.
They have time on their hands during the weekends, so they take up hobbies and explore new activities.
He is in between jobs at the moment, so he has time on his hands to travel and pursue personal projects.
After completing the project ahead of schedule, he had time on his hands to take a short vacation.
They finished their work early, so they had time on their hands to catch up on their favorite TV series.

senses.definition: to go through a process in a way that is natural, normal, or expected

examples.x: The flu typically runs its course within a week, and rest is usually the best treatment.
They often let their children's creativity take its course without imposing too many rules.
The investigation is ongoing, and they are letting the legal process take its course.
She is allowing the healing process to run its course before resuming her training.
The storm ran its course, leaving a trail of destruction in its wake.
Despite their efforts to save the relationship, it eventually took its course and ended.

senses.definition: to be too soon to determine what something will result in, particularly because it is still in its early stages of development

examples.x: At that time, it was early days, and we were still figuring out our strategy.
They had just started their business, so it was early days for them.
By next month, it will still be early days for the new venture, and we'll be learning as we go.
If they continue investing in research and development, it could pay off down the line, but it's early days yet.
It's early days yet, so we'll have to wait and see how it unfolds.
